title: Generalization of Prigogine’s theorem for the case of full differential of entropy
author: Shapovalov, Kazakov
materials: In practice the entrostat satisfies the following condition: |ΔS|/S>>|ΔSr|/Sr, where ΔS and ΔSr are the changes of entropy of the studied system and external system (caused by their interaction), respectively. For example, in physical systems the entrostat is a thermostat. Therefore, the role of the entrostat is played by an external system in which the change in entropy can be neglected in comparison with the change in entropy of the system under entrostat affect.

(Let us recall that degree of openness is understood to be the system parameter which quantitatively characterizes the value of impact of an entrostat on the system [[7], [8], [9]]. For example, in thermodynamics when describing the process of thermal conductivity in a plane-parallel plate located between two thermostats, the degree of openness of the system is the temperature difference between these thermostats.)
For this purpose we take into account that in Prigogine’s model the thermostat maintains a constant temperature difference, i.e. it is an entrostat. Therefore, we can apply SCEC.

Let us consider two cases (only for systems under the influence of the entrostat).Case 1Suppose that the degree of openness of the system has increased, for example, the temperature difference between the vessels has increased. According to the fourth point of SCEC, the system will tend towards a state with lower entropy. Hence, σ 0 and P−=−P>0. It is easy to see that previous arguments remain valid for the new variables. Really,σ−=−dsdt=d(−s)dt=∑XnIn,where Xn = ∂(−s)/∂an ; an – local thermodynamic parameter. As a result we come to a positive square form for Xn :σ−=L11X12+2L21X1X2+L22X22>0

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Recall that the entrostat is the external system that does not change its entropy when it affects the system under investigation [7,8].

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: The main advantage of introducing the concept of an entrostat is that it allows for exclusion of the external environment when describing an open system. Indeed, since the parameters of the entrostat do not change when interacting with the system, all the variables are only related to the system itself. The latter fact made it possible to formulate and prove the so-called statistical criterion for entropy change (SCEC) in an open system [[7], [8], [9]]. Without enumerating all the provisions of this criterion, we confine ourselves to five that are relevant to the subject of this paper (see Additional information):

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 1. The system, which is under entrostat affect, has a special value of entropy, which is called critical.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 2. If the entropy of the system is more than the critical value, then processes that decrease entropy (dS 0) predominate. When the critical value is reached, these processes balance each other, and the state of the system becomes stationary.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 3. The critical value of the entropy of the system uniquely corresponds to the degree of openness of the system. In order to increase or decrease the critical value of entropy, it is necessary to reduce or increase the degree of openness of the system in relation to entrostat, respectively.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 4. If openness degree increases, then the critical value of entropy decreases. According to the second point above, the entropy of the system has to decrease (dS<0) to the new value corresponding to a new degree of openness.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: 5. If openness degree decreases, then the critical value of entropy increases. According to the second point above, the entropy of the system has to increase (dS>0) to the new value corresponding to a new degree of openness.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Generalized theorem on the minimum rate of change of entropy near the stationary state of the system

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Let us apply this criterion to a system [1] for which Prigogine's theorem of a minimum production of entropy has been proven.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Using the conception of Local Equilibrium, we introduce the notation of known values:

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: s=dS/dV – local entropy, i.e. entropy per unit volume (recall that s=ρsˆ, where ρ is the density; sˆ=dS/(ρdV) is the specific local entropy, i.e. the entropy per unit mass); V is volume and S is the entropy of the system;

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: σ = ds/dt – rate of change of local entropy (according to (1) σ=σi+σe where σi – the local production of entropy);

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: P=dS/dt=∫σ dV – rate of change in the entropy of the system.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Pay attention; in all of these expressions dS is the full differential of entropy.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Proceeding as in [1], we assume that the linear components of the processes prevail near the stationary state. This assumption allows us to apply the following known equations: σ=∑XnIn, In=∑LnkXk and Lnk=Lkn, where Xn is a generalized force, In is a generalized flow, Lnk are kinetic coefficients. Our calculations differ from that ones [1] by the fact that we use the rate of change of local entropy σ instead of local entropy production.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Consider a system consisting of two vessels separated by a partition. These vessels have different temperatures and composition of the substances inside them. A constant temperature difference is maintained between the vessels. The partition is removed and, at the same time, two streams appear in the system: the flow I1 arising due to temperature differences, and flow I2 arising due to the unequal composition of the vessels. As a result, after some time the system goes into a stationary state. The flow I1 corresponds to the generalized force X1. The diffusive flow I2 corresponds to the generalized force X2.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Let us remind that we consider the rate of local change of entropy σ instead of local production of entropy σi. It is obvious that σ|st=0 (“st” means “stationary state”). Then it follows from the equation σ=∑XnIn that X1|st=0 i.e. after the disappearance of the septum, this force gradually decreases to zero. Consequently, X1 is a variable (like X2 force), which slightly differs our line of reasoning from [1]. In the absence of X1 force, the flow I1 disappears, i.e. I1|st=0. Also I2|st=0, since diffusion stops when the system reaches a stationary state.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: As a result, we come to the ratios similar to those found in [1] except that they contain σ instead of σi and both generalized forces are variable:(4)σ=I1X1+I2X2I1=L11X1+L12X2, I2=L21X1+L22X2σ=L11X12+2L21X1X2+L22X22=L11X12+2L12X1X2+L22X22.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: As can be seen from (4), σ is a function of two variables: X1 and X2. To determine its extremum, we find the corresponding derivatives:(4a)∂σ∂X2=2(L21X1+L22X2)=2I2;∂σ∂X1=2(L11X1+L12X2)=2I1;(5)∂2σ∂X22=2L22=A;

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Since I2|st=0 and I1|st=0 (see above), then in (4a) both derivatives are equal to zero in the stationary state.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: We got that in linear processes in a stationary state, the rate of change of the local entropy σ, and consequently the entropy change rate P, can have an extremum.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: To determine the existence and type of extremum, it is necessary to find the sign of discriminant D=AC−B2=4(L22L11−L212) and sign A=2L22 (or sign C=2L11).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction:
[see (4)]. We will find eigenvalues for the matrix of coefficients of this square form:λ1=12L11+L22+(L11−L22)2+4L122 >0;λ2=12L11+L22−(L11−L22)2+4L122 >0.Here, we have taken into account that the signs of the eigenvalues coincide with the sign of the quadratic form. We find from the second inequality that L11L22>L122. From where it follows that a) both coefficients have the same signs and b) D>0, i.e.
σ− has an extremum in a stationary state. Adding the inequalities we obtain L11+L22>0. Therefore, both coefficients are positive and A>0.

We will find eigenvalues for the matrix of coefficients of this square form:λ1=12L11+L22+(L11−L22)2+4L122 >0;λ2=12L11+L22−(L11−L22)2+4L122 >0.Here, we have taken into account that the signs of the eigenvalues coincide with the sign of the quadratic form. We find from the second inequality that L11L22>L122. From where it follows that
both coefficients have the same signs and

D>0, i.e.
σ− has an extremum in a stationary state. Adding the inequalities we obtain L11+L22>0. Therefore, both coefficients are positive and A>0.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Thus, in the stationary state the extremum of the functions σ− and P− is a minimum. Mathematically, this statement can be written as(6)dP−dt≤0(‘=’ corresponds to a stationary state).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: We will call value of P− the rate of reduction (decrease) of entropy of the system.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: It follows from (6) that in linear processes, as the system approaches the stationary state, this rate should decrease.Case 2Let us assume that the degree of openness of the system has decreased, for example, we have reduced the difference in temperature between the vessels. According to the fourth SCEC point, the system will tend to a state with more entropy: σ>0 and P>0. The quadratic form in the expression for σ is positive. It means that we come to the inequality(7)dPdt≤0(‘=’ corresponds to a stationary state).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: Inequalities (6) and (7) are a mathematical expression of the generalized theorem on the minimum rate of change of entropy, of an open system under the influence of an entrostat. Its formulation states that, in linear processes (with a constant value of the entrostat influence), as the system approaches the stationary state, the rate of change of its entropy decreases and reaches a minimum in the stationary state [9]. If processes that decrease entropy predominate in the system, then the rate of decrease of entropy decreases. If the processes that increase entropy prevail in the system, the rate of increase of entropy decreases.

steps.title: allows for changes in the sign...
steps.instruction: allows for changes in the sign of the second (at least) order time derivative of entropy. This indicates the theoretical possibility of the appearance of entropic oscillations in a neighborhood of the stationary state [9].
